GEN_1_2: The Republic of Benin exercises complete and exclusive sovereignty over the airspace above its territory and territorial waters. Foreign aircraft and their crews must comply with Benin laws and regulations during overflight, landing, stay and departure. Except in the case of a forced landing, foreign aircraft operating to/from Benin must make their first landing and final departure at a customs aerodrome; the Civil Aviation Authority may, with Customs agreement, authorize a first landing at an aerodrome without customs services. For operational clarity: a State aircraft is any aircraft used for military, customs or police service or any civil aircraft used by a public service; other aircraft are civil aircraft; an aerodrome is any defined area on land or water, including buildings, installations and equipment, intended for arrival, departure and surface movement of aircraft; a Public Transport Air Service carries passengers, cargo or mail for remuneration; Aerial Work comprises flights conducted to perform work, including aerial photography/topographical survey, agricultural or public‑health dispersals, advertising (towed banners, signs or loudspeakers), exploitation of soil/subsoil/sea bottoms or atmospheric phenomena and control of locusts or migratory birds, and flight instruction in schools and training centres; a Private Flight is any flight not falling within Public Transport or Aerial Work; a Public Transport Scheduled Air Service operates a series of public flights between predesignated points on approved routes according to published timetables with systematic frequency and regularity, while a Non‑Scheduled Public Transport Air Service lacks those scheduled characteristics; Public Transport services are international when they use the airspace of two or more States; a Non‑commercial stop is a landing for purposes other than embarking or disembarking passengers, cargo or mail, and a Commercial stop is a landing for embarking or disembarking passengers, cargo or mail. Foreign civil aircraft may operate international scheduled public transport services in Benin only under international agreements between Benin and the State of registration or under a special temporary authorization; requests for authorizations or special temporary authorizations must be submitted to the Civil Aviation Authority and must include the designated airline, aircraft type and registration, and the service timetable (route, frequency and exact arrival and departure times), and applicants will be notified of the decision.

GEN_1_2: Only normally registered aircraft may land at aerodromes under Customs control. Upon crossing the Burkina frontier they must follow the most direct air route and may land only at customs aerodromes; forced landings are subject to special control measures. Jettisoning or unloading cargo in flight is strictly prohibited except to save the aerodrome. Aircraft are exempt from customs on entry provided the Customs Service is notified of aircraft characteristics, date of passage and exact route. Exempt aircraft may remain on customs aerodromes only as long as necessary to complete stopover formalities and to disembark or embark passengers and cargo; in the event of a technical incident requiring prolonged parking they must remain under Customs surveillance. On departure aircraft may leave Burkina aerodromes without formalities but must follow the most direct air route to the next stopover. There are no immigration formalities. No aircraft disinfection is required; responsibility lies with the transport airlines.

GEN_1_2: Airspace above the Central African Republic is under national sovereignty in accordance with Article 1 of the Convention on International Civil Aviation (Chicago, 7 December 1944). All aircraft conducting international flights that intend to use or use a national aerodrome must perform their first landing and final take-off in the Central African Republic at a customs aerodrome, except by special derogation granted by the Minister of Defence. Overflight of CAR territory by foreign state or civil aircraft, with or without stopover, requires prior government authorization; overflight and landing authorizations are issued by the Minister of Defence after consultation with the Minister of Transport (Civil Aviation) and the Minister of Public Security and Territory Administration. Requests for authorization must be sent to the Minister of Defence by telex, radio message or other appropriate means at least 72 hours before the intended flight, unless an express derogation is granted, and must include aircraft type, registration and call sign, owner and operator details, commander name and nationality and number of crew, flight purpose, loading details (passenger numbers, cargo weight and type, origin and final destination), planned route with entry and exit points and any stopover aerodromes, and dates and times for all flight stages including any changes. The Minister of Defence will forward requests without delay to the Minister of Public Security and the Minister of Transport for advice to be issued within 48 hours and will inform the Minister of Foreign Affairs; the Minister of Defence will notify the applicant of the decision, and absence of a reply within 72 hours of receipt shall be deemed a refusal.

GEN_1_2: Foreign aircraft may not overfly or land in Côte d'Ivoire without authorization; aircraft operating international routes must use an international airport on arrival and departure and must follow assigned air routes when crossing the border or territorial waters, and all laws and regulations relating to the entry or exit of passengers, crew or goods (immigration, passports, customs, health) must be observed. An international airport is any airport designated as an entry and exit point for international air traffic where customs, identity control, public health, veterinary, phytosanitary and similar formalities are carried out. No special authorized arrangements exist for landings on aerodromes that are not international airports; in the event of an emergency landing at a non‑international aerodrome the commander shall immediately notify the local Customs, Police and Health authorities by any available means and shall not disembark passengers or cargo. Article 15 of the Convention on International Civil Aviation is applied in Côte d'Ivoire. Movement of persons and vehicles on aerodromes is governed by Decree No. 2012‑837 of 8 August 2012, Decree No. 2012‑836 of 8 August 2012 as amended by Decree No. 2022‑163 of 9 March 2022, and Order No. 423/MT/CAB/ of 29 May 2012; aerodrome land is divided into public and restricted zones, access to restricted zones is limited to persons with valid transport tickets for flights for which check‑in has commenced, boarding passes for flights being processed, valid admission passes, or crew/professional ID accompanied by a general declaration for the flight, and technical vehicles or special equipment may enter, circulate and park in the restricted zone only with an authorization materialized by a sticker or one‑off pass displayed visibly on the front of the vehicle or equipment.

GEN_1_2: The national civil aviation regulations of the Republic of Mali apply to all aircraft conducting entry, exit, overflight or landing in Malian territory. All aircraft arriving in or departing from Mali must land at or depart from a customs aerodrome unless expressly exempted by the regulations in force (see Part "Aerodromes" 10 AD 1.3‑1). Foreign operators of international scheduled services to or from Mali must be duly designated by a State party to a bilateral or multilateral air transport agreement signed by the Government of Mali and must hold authorization to operate international scheduled services in accordance with national legislation; applications for such authorization must be submitted to the Ministry in charge of Transport (Directorate of Civil Aviation, P.O. Box 227, Bamako, Republic of Mali). Aircraft used for overflight of Mali or for operations to Malian public aerodromes are only required to provide the flight plan as prior notice. For aircraft clearance on entry and departure, the required aircraft documents must be completed in accordance with ICAO models, may be in English or French, must be legible, one copy of the general declaration must be signed by the pilot commander, and no visa will be required.

GEN_1_2: Airspace over Mauritania, including territorial waters, is under the complete and exclusive sovereignty of the Islamic Republic of Mauritania. Overflights of Mauritanian territory by foreign aircraft, with or without landings, are subject to applicable international conventions, Law No. 78‑009 of 18 January 1978 on Civil Aviation and its implementing texts, and this decree. Foreign aircraft and their crews must comply with Mauritanian laws and regulations during overflight, landing, stay and departure. Except in the case of forced landings, foreign aircraft must carry out their first landing and final departure at a customs aerodrome; however, pursuant to Article 62 of the Civil Aviation Law the Ministry responsible for Civil Aviation, with the agreement of the Customs Administration, may authorize certain foreign aircraft to make their first landing at an aerodrome without customs services.

GEN_1_2: The Republic of Cabo Verde exercises sovereignty over the airspace covering the archipelago and adjacent territorial waters; all flights into, from, or over this airspace and any landings or departures shall comply with the regulations below and apply on a reciprocal basis. Aircraft landing in or departing from Cabo Verde must first land or finally depart from an international airport (see AD 1.3 Index to Aerodromes and Heliports); landings at aerodromes require prior permission from the Civil Aviation Agency (AAC) via http://siga.aac.cv/ext/fpr. Filing a flight plan specifying the intended arrival or departure aerodrome is mandatory. Operations to/from Level 3 Coordinated Airports (GVBA, GVNP and GVAC) require a slot request submitted prior to operation (see ENR 1.9.3.3).
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Aircraft registered in ICAO States, subject to reciprocal treatment, do not require prior permission to enter the Republic of Cabo Verde; filing a flight plan constitutes adequate advance notification provided it is transmitted so it is received by the aviation authorities at least two hours before arrival and landing is carried out at a designated international airport. Permits may be issued for a 48- or 72-hour window when requested in the application. For Private Non‑Commercial Flights, landing permits are required and applications must be submitted to AAC with sufficient time to allow a response before commencement of flight; for urgent applications on weekends, after 16:00 of every working day and on national public holidays, address applications to GVACYNYX, Attention Duty Airport Operations Supervisor, Tel/Fax: +238 241 1309. For private flights operated by aircraft registered in non‑ICAO States, or where reciprocity may not exist, special permission in addition to filing a flight plan is required and must be applied for through diplomatic channels.

airports.AD2.3 Remarks: Prior coordination (3 HR in advance) through the AD administration is required for non scheduled flights. Outside HR available on request (24 HR in advance).

GEN_1_2: Foreign commercial aircraft operating scheduled air services to or within Chad under bilateral or multilateral air transport agreements between the Republic of Chad and the State of registration are not required to provide any notification for overflight of Chadian territory or for landing on a Chadian customs aerodrome other than that contained in the filed flight plan; subject to reciprocity, the same facilitation applies to aircraft of national carriers of States party to the Convention of Saint-Louis. All flights over Chad and landings at Chadian aerodromes must comply with Chadian civil aviation regulations, including rules for desert zone overflight. Any aircraft operating on a Chadian aerodrome must perform its first landing or final take-off at one of the following customs airports: N'DJAMENA, SARH, MOUNDOU, ABECHE, FAYA LARGEAU.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: General aviation aircraft are subject to the same rules as those specified in Article 5 for non‑scheduled commercial flights. Any temporary restriction to this decree will be notified to pilots by NOTAM. The facilities and privileges described in the preceding articles may be temporarily withdrawn from aircraft of any State against which such measures are taken, either by an inter‑African organization if Chad has associated itself with that decision, or by the Council of Ministers for security reasons. Decree 179 of 26 September 1963 remains in force.

GEN_1_2: Foreign-registered aircraft may operate over the territory of the Union of the Comoros only where that right is granted by an international convention or by a special authorization whose validity shall not exceed twelve months (Article 1). The first landing in the Union of the Comoros of any foreign-registered aircraft must be at a customs aerodrome except as provided in Article 172 of the Code of Civil Aviation (Article 2). Requests for authorization for non‑commercial overflight and/or landing must be submitted to the National Agency of Civil Aviation and Meteorology in writing at least ten days before the scheduled flight and by fax, email or AFTN at least three days before arrival, and shall include aircraft type and registration, airworthiness certificate type and validity, insurance policy references and validity, flight number, owner and/or operator, pilot‑in‑command name, crew and passenger numbers and identities and status of any persons on board, complete itinerary with entry and exit points and estimated arrival/departure times, nature/quantity/weight of cargo, charterer and consignee, and the purpose of the flight/landing; deadlines do not apply to medical evacuation flights or safety emergency landings (Article 3). Authorizations for civil aircraft carrying troops or military cargo must be submitted via diplomatic channels in accordance with Articles 10 and 11 and include the information required by Article 3 (Article 4). Authorizations for single (punctual) flights are valid for seventy‑two hours unless otherwise specified (Article 5). Authorizations to operate series of non‑scheduled commercial flights inbound to or outbound from the Union of the Comoros must be submitted in writing (correspondence, fax or AFTN) at least forty‑five days before the first flight and must include the Article‑3 information plus a copy of the air operator’s permit, copies of insurance, airworthiness, registration and radio‑equipment operation certificates for the aircraft to be used, number of flights, rotation and per‑seat fares, period, charterer, local partner, and name and full address of the company’s representative in the Union of the Comoros (Article 6). Requests for aerial work operations must include, in addition to Articles 3 and 6 requirements, an introductory letter from the responsible official organization, areas and altitudes of operation, specific equipment carried, and any other obtained authorizations (Article 7). Operation of scheduled commercial flights is governed by Articles 324, 331, 332, 333 and 334 of the Code of Civil Aviation and by international agreements or conventions; operators must respect the prescribed submission and processing deadlines (Article 8). Requests for authorization must comply with the regulations in force (Article 9).

GEN_1_2: International flights into, from, or over Rwanda are subject to current Rwanda civil aviation regulations, which correspond in all essentials to the Standards and Recommended Practices of ICAO Annex 9. Aircraft flying into or departing Rwanda must make their first landing or final departure at an international aerodrome (see AIP RWANDA AD 1.3, AD 2). All aircraft operating in or over Rwanda airspace must have Rwanda authorization or clearance, submit a copy of the flight plan and the departure message, and establish contact with the appropriate ATS Unit.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: All flights within Rwandan airspace and at Rwandan aerodromes require special permission, which must be negotiated through diplomatic channels.

GEN_1_2: All foreign aircraft must comply with customs, immigration and health formalities: first landing or final take-off for entry/departure must be made at a designated customs aerodrome (LOMÉ/Gnassingbé Eyadema or NIAMTOUGOU) and a General Declaration and Cargo Manifest must be submitted; aircraft in transit or on technical stop must perform their first landing or final take-off at a customs aerodrome and submit a General Declaration; immigration requires a General Declaration and Cargo Manifest for arrival, departure and transit, while health formalities require a General Declaration and Cargo Manifest for arrival and departure and no health formality applies to aircraft in transit. Overflight and landing permission must be obtained from the Directorate of Civil Aviation at least 72 hours before the flight; requests must state the aircraft type and registration, operator name, departure aerodrome, date and time of arrival, purpose of flight, commander name and nationality, number and names of crew, number of passengers, nature and quantity of cargo and name and address of the cargo recipient; requests for landings between 0000 UTC and 0600 UTC should be avoided. Permission to overfly or land is granted to aircraft of ICAO member States subject to air navigation regulations, except state and military aircraft and scheduled international services that cannot rely on special bilateral agreements; state and foreign military aircraft require a special authorization from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and non‑scheduled commercial flights (remunerated or on lease) require authorization from the Ministry of Public Works, Mines, Transport, Posts and Telecommunications in consultation with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs unless no boarding or unloading of passengers, cargo or mail occurs during the stopover. The first landing must be at an aerodrome authorized to receive international arrivals; landing at a Togolese aerodrome other than LOMÉ requires a 12‑hour prior notice to local customs, immigration and health authorities or to civil aviation authorities in Lomé by any available means. Competent authorities may inspect aircraft, certificates and crew documents on arrival and departure and may restrict or temporarily prohibit overflight of all or part of the territory for security reasons.

GEN_1_2: All international flights into, from, or over Somali territory are subject to the current Somalia Civil Aviation Regulations (SOMCARs), which correspond in all essential respects to the Standards and Recommended Practices of ICAO Annex 9. Aircraft operating into or departing from Somalia must make their first landing at, or final departure from, an airport where customs and immigration services are available, as listed in AD 1.3.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Operators intending to conduct non‑scheduled flights transiting across Somalia or making non‑traffic stops must obtain prior landing/overflying permission from the Somali Civil Aviation Authority (SCAA). Operators intending non‑scheduled flights into Somalia for the purpose of taking on or discharging passengers, cargo or mail must apply to the SCAA not less than twenty‑four hours in advance of the intended landing; applications must be submitted to Somali Civil Aviation Authority (SCAA), Adan Adde International Airport, Mogadishu, Somalia, Tel: +2521853675, +2521853676, Email: scaa@scaa.gov.so, mnof@scaa.gov.so. The application must include the name and address of the operator, aircraft type, aircraft callsign, registration mark, nature of flight, date and place of origin of the flight and the complete route itinerary including dates and times (UTC).

GEN_1_2: International flights into, from, or over the Republic of Armenia are subject to the Republic of Armenia's civil aviation regulations, which correspond in all essentials to the Standards and Recommended Practices of ICAO Annex 9. Aircraft operating into or out of Armenian territory must make their first landing at, or final departure from, an international aerodrome (see AIP Armenia AD 1.3 and AD 2), and flights of foreign aircraft in Armenian airspace must be conducted under applicable international multilateral agreements, bilateral Air Services agreements or protocols between aeronautical authorities, or under special permission from the relevant authorized body.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Single non-scheduled transits of foreign civil aircraft through the Republic of Armenia without landing require no special permission; only a filed flight plan is required. Single non-scheduled air transport operations to, from or within Armenia require permission from the Ministry of Territorial Administration and Infrastructure. To obtain permission the air operator or an applicant who is not an air operator must submit an application at least 72 hours before the flight via aviation.e-gov.am and include the name of the air operator, place of business and all contact numbers; flight number; state of registry; aircraft type, nationality and registration marks and maximum take-off weight (MTOW); purpose of flight; type of traffic (passenger, cargo, combined, etc.); flight route, date and time of operation and departure/arrival aerodrome; load of the aircraft including number of passengers with full names, weights and the nature of cargo; and receiving party names and contact numbers, and attach copies of the Air Operator Certificate with annexes or operations specifications; Airworthiness Certificate (and Airworthiness Review Certificate if available) and Registration Certificate for the aircraft intended for the operation; insurance certificates for passengers, baggage, cargo and third party liabilities; and the certificate of the pilot-in-command if the applicant is not an air operator. A national Restricted Permit to Fly or national Restricted Certificate of Airworthiness issued for historical aircraft under EU Regulation (EC) No 216/2008 Annex II Article a(i), originally factory-manufactured and previously holding an ICAO-compliant Certificate of Airworthiness and subsequently operated under national rules of an ECAC Member State, may be accepted only for aircraft with MTOW less than 5 700 kg and operated in non-commercial flights. If urgency prevents submission at least 72 hours in advance, the applicant must indicate reasons for late submission via e-mail or in the application. Applications for such permits may be submitted online at aviation.e-gov.am or via email to armairtransport@gmail.com or cds@armats.am, telephone +37410 515167 or +37410283429, AFS: UDDDZXZX and UDDUYAYX. Permissions granted by the Ministry of Territorial Administration and Infrastructure are valid for 48 hours from the time indicated in the permission. For two or more non-scheduled flights to the Republic of Armenia refer to point 2.1.2. For training and special flights (including balloons, gliders, hang-gliders, paragliders, airships and remotely piloted aircraft/RPAS) the applicant must submit an application and cover letter at least 3 working days before the operation by email to gdca@gdca.am or in paper form to the Civil Aviation Committee of Armenia (CAC), CAC Building, Zvartnots Airport, 0042 Yerevan, Republic of Armenia; CAC contacts: working hours +37460 434250 / +37460 434205 and +37410 280722; non-working hours 24/7 +37460 434295 / +37460 434296 and +37410 283429; see application forms at http://www.aviation.am/storage/files/editor/training%20flights.pdf and http://www.aviation.am/storage/files/editor/special%20flights.pdf and GDCA order at http://www.arlis.am/DocumentView.aspx?DocID=108161. Documentary requirements for clearance of aircraft are the same as for scheduled flights.

GEN_1_2: All flights landing in, departing from or overflying the territory of Bahrain must comply with Bahrain Civil Aviation Law and Regulations and must provide a valid company mailing address for invoicing; failure to do so will result in rejection of the relevant approval request.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: For non‑scheduled traffic stops by AOC holders in Bahrain, submit a request through AL‑NAWRAS (GEN 1.2.1.1.1.1) with a minimum notice period of one week; required documents in addition to GEN 1.2.1.1.1.2 information are: Air Operator’s Certificate reflecting the aircraft registration mark(s), Certificate of Registration, Certificate of Airworthiness, Insurance Certificate with full liability coverage (see GEN 1.2.1.1.10), Noise Certificate, Aircraft Radio Station License, ACAS II/TCAS Certificate (mandatory within Bahrain airspace), Basic Area Navigation (B‑RNAV) Certificate if reequipped, and RVSM Certificate when operating above 29,000 ft; for leased aircraft also provide a copy of the lease agreement approved by the State of Registry and the State of Operator, a conformity statement from the State of Registry confirming it remains responsible for safety oversight, and evidence showing the state responsible for safety oversight where a transfer of functions has occurred. Subject to applicable rules, aircraft registered in ICAO contracting States may overfly or make non‑traffic stops without prior overflight permission provided the State is a Chicago Convention signatory; diplomatic or military flights (e.g. State VIP, transport of ammunitions) require approval per GEN 1.2.1.1.7.1. Applications for non‑traffic stops must be submitted through AL‑NAWRAS with a minimum 48‑hour notice for administrative and operational reasons and must include, in addition to GEN 1.2.1.1.1.2 information, the date(s) of flight and type of cargo (if any). Operators of aircraft registered in States not signatory to the ICAO Convention must obtain prior approval to overfly or land in Bahrain: a minimum notice period of one week is required for AOC non‑scheduled traffic stops and for non‑ICAO State overflights, and a minimum notice period of 48 hours is required for private/general aviation non‑ICAO State operations when specified. Documentary requirements for clearance are the same as for scheduled flights (see GEN 1.2.1.1.2.4 and GEN 1.2.1.1.4 as applicable). Arrival/departure aircraft document distribution: Immigration requires one General Declaration and one Passenger Manifest (Cargo Manifest not required), Health requires one General Declaration and one Passenger Manifest (Cargo Manifest not required), Customs requires one General Declaration and one Cargo Manifest; total required copies per movement are: General Declaration 4, Passenger Manifest 2, Cargo Manifest 1. Public health measures applied to these aircraft are the same as for scheduled flights, and private/general aviation operators wishing to land for taking on or discharging passengers, cargo or mail must apply through AL‑NAWRAS with a minimum 48‑hour notice and provide purpose of flight, passenger and company name, name of VIP (if any), type of cargo (if any), and hosting company and/or contact in Bahrain; overflying or refuelling/crew rest stops for private/general aviation do not require prior permission except for diplomatic or military operations, which require approval per GEN 1.2.1.1.7.1.

GEN_1_2: An international flight is any flight on which an aircraft crosses the state border of Georgia and that of another State. International flights of foreign aircraft in Georgian airspace must be conducted under applicable international air‑service treaties and only with special single‑flight permits issued by the Georgian Civil Aviation Agency or obtained via the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Georgia. All civil aircraft operating in Georgian airspace must carry insurance meeting these minimums: passenger — for aircraft with MTOW less than 2700 kg or 2700 kg — 100000 SDR; for aircraft with MTOW more than 2700 kg — 250000 SDR; baggage — 1519 SDR; cargo — 26 SDR per kg; third‑party legal liability minimums (Million SDR) by MTOW: <500 kg — 0.75; <1000 kg — 1.5; <2700 kg — 3; <6000 kg — 7; <12000 kg — 18; <25000 kg — 80; <50000 kg — 150; <200000 kg — 300; <500000 kg — 500; ≥500000 kg — 700. Flights of foreign civil aircraft in Georgian airspace shall use international airways and airports designated for international flights, and when operating to a domestic airport not designated for international flights the aircraft must make its first landing and last departure in Georgia at an airport open for international flights.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Non‑scheduled flights transiting the airspace of Georgia without landing at Georgian airports require no prior permission. Non‑scheduled flights intending to operate into Georgian airports must apply to the Georgian Civil Aviation Agency not later than five working days before commencement of the flight; the application must be prepared as specified in Para 4.1.3 and submitted to the addresses in Para 2.1.3. The Georgian Civil Aviation Agency issues a single permission number per request, valid for 24 hours from the date stated in the application, and operators must insert this permission number in Item 18 of the FPL. Documentary requirements for aircraft clearance are the same as for scheduled flights. Random flights of foreign state aircraft (including conveyance of heads of state and government, delegations headed by them, Ministers of Foreign Affairs and Defence, and foreign military and state aircraft) require permission obtained via diplomatic channels from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Georgia and must be requested via diplomatic channels to the addresses in Para 2.1.3 not later than five working days before the flight; the application must be prepared as specified in Para 4.1.3 and shall include the airline name (three‑letter ICAO designator), state of aircraft registration and address, aircraft type and modification, revenue capacity and MTOW, registration marks of main and alternate aircraft and the aircraft owner (operator or lessee), pilot‑in‑command name, number of crew and their nationalities, purpose of the flight and probable organisations or persons in Georgia involved, flight category (governmental, special, charter, technical or other) with rank and name of head of delegation for governmental flights, flight number and radiotelephone/radiotelegraphy call signs, aircraft load (number of passengers, weight and nature of cargo), date of flight and UTC schedule with points of commercial and technical landings and the amount of aviation fuel required at Georgian airports, the airways, points and estimated times of entry into and leaving Georgian airspace, the insurance company name and address, insurance policy number and amount of liability cover for passengers, baggage, cargo and third parties not being transported on the aircraft, and payment details for services and state charges including the payer’s bank and bank account numbers.

GEN_1_2: International flights into, from, or over the Tel‑Aviv FIR are subject to current Israeli civil aviation regulations (see GEN 1.6). Aircraft operating to or from the Tel‑Aviv FIR must make their first landing at, or last departure from, an international aerodrome (see AD 2.2, AD 2.5 & AD 2.7). Operators intending to file any such aerodrome as an alternate must notify the Aviation Security Operation Center (ASOC), Israeli MOT Security Department, by fax (+972 3 9599808) or e‑mail (asoc@mot.gov.il) at least five working days before the start of each IATA season. Flights to or from the Tel‑Aviv FIR must originate from or be destined to an aerodrome published in an AIC.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Operators intending to operate commercial non‑scheduled (charter) flights into the State of Israel to embark or disembark passengers, cargo or mail must obtain an operating permit from the Civil Aviation Authority (CAAI) International Relations & Air Transport Division; submit applications to: International Relations & Air Transport Division, Civil Aviation Authority, GOLAN Building, Golan St., P.O. BOX 1101, Airport‑City 7019900, ISRAEL, Tel: +972 3 9774523/551/521, Fax: +972 3 9774594. Submit permit applications at least four days prior to the intended landing for operators proposing up to a maximum of four charter flights within eight consecutive weeks; operators proposing more than four charter flights within eight consecutive weeks (traffic program) must submit applications at least 30 days prior to the intended landing/effective date of the traffic program. Applications may be submitted by an authorized organization or authorized person and must follow Directive AT.1.1.402 and include Forms ATF 1.1.402A (Application for operation of Charter Flights), ATF 1.1.400A (Commercial specifications), ATF 1.1.400B (Operational specifications) and, for passenger charters, ATF 1.1.400C (Commitment to appoint an airport representative). Include a copy of the signed charter agreement between the tour operator/charterer and the air operator; documents demonstrating adequate insurance coverage including third‑party liability; if cargo is to be carried, confirmation from the State of Operator aviation authority that the operator is authorized to transport general cargo and/or dangerous goods as applicable; a list of aircraft to be used on services to and from Israel signed by the competent authority of the State of the Operator or, alternatively, the aircraft certificates: registration, noise, airworthiness and radio station authorization; and, if applicable, an application to operate wet‑leased aircraft. The CAAI International Relations & Air Transport Division will forward the application to the Aviation Security Operation Center (ASOC) of the Israeli MOT Security Department for security approval and additional documents may be requested during that process. All applications must follow Directive AT.1.1.402 and use the prescribed forms available on the CAAI website (https://www.gov.il/he/Departments/DynamicCollectors/civil-aviation-authority-directives?skip=0&number=AT.1.1.402); any changes to the submitted data must be notified in advance to the CAAI International Relations & Air Transport Division. Documentary requirements for aircraft clearance are the same as for commercial scheduled flights. Prior permission is not required for commercial non‑scheduled flights registered in Contracting States of the Chicago Convention that have diplomatic relations with Israel when overflying Israel or making non‑traffic technical stops; prior permission is required for such flights by aircraft registered in States that are not Contracting States or that do not have diplomatic relations and must be obtained per the procedure in paragraph 3.1. Regardless, prior notification for all commercial flights must be submitted to ASOC by Fax +97239599808 or by e‑mail to asoc@mot.gov.il at least five working days prior to the effective date of the flight.

GEN_1_2: A flight that crosses the state border of the Kyrgyz Republic is an international flight; international flights in Kyrgyz airspace are classified as scheduled or nonscheduled (charter) and may be conducted only on the basis of issued permissions under applicable international treaties and aviation authority agreements, permissions to operate single flights granted by MC ATM of the Kyrgyz Republic, or approvals for single flights legalized through the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Kyrgyz Republic. Operators must, as a condition of the obtained permission, submit a flight plan to MC ATM of the Kyrgyz Republic and other addresses listed in ENR 1.10 of this AIP in accordance with the procedure in ENR 1.10.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Operators from states with which the Kyrgyz Republic has international agreements, and all airspace users intending to operate in Kyrgyz airspace, must submit a request in the prescribed form and within the prescribed time to MC ATM of KR to obtain permission; the issued permission is valid for 72 hours. For international flights by foreign carriers requiring customs and border control, the first landing and the final departure from Kyrgyz territory must be at airports with full customs/border facilities: Manas, Karakol, Osh, Issyk-Kul; mobile border and customs points may be arranged by operator request and SCAA (GAGA) permission for aerodromes not listed and for specially equipped international mountaineering and tourism camp sites. A request for a single (charter) flight must be submitted to MC ATM of the Kyrgyz Republic not later than 3 working days using Form N (sample GEN 1.2-8 and GEN 1.2-9). Foreign carriers operating to domestic Kyrgyz airports must carry an escort navigator (a navigator who is a citizen of the Kyrgyz Republic holding an escort navigator's certificate) on board. To obtain permission, the operator must provide: the name of the aircraft operator; aircraft type and registration mark; date and time of arrival and departure at/from the given airport; point(s) of embarkation/disembarkation of passengers and/or loading/unloading of cargo abroad; purpose of flight, number of passengers and/or kind and quantity of cargo; name, address and kind of activity of the charterer, if any; and documents on insurance or other provision of liability for injury to third parties and other aircraft. Requests for flights conveying Heads of State, governments, their delegations, Ministers of Foreign Affairs or Defence shall be submitted to SCAA of KR and MC ATM of KR through the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Kyrgyz Republic. Requests for non-civil flights shall be submitted to SCAA of KR and MC ATM of KR, to the Ministry of Defence and to the relevant Agency through the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Requests for single (charter) flights of aircraft of a state with which the Kyrgyz Republic has no international agreement shall be submitted to SCAA of KR and MC ATM of KR. Emergency flights for saving life (medical, search and rescue) may be conducted without prior request but require mandatory coordination and subsequent transmission of movement messages to MC ATM of the Kyrgyz Republic by telephone +996312 393552, fax +996312 393573, AFS UСFMZDZX, UСFMZDZW, and email gcz.ovd@bk.ru, atfmu@caa.kg. For each request MC ATM of KR issues a registration number of permission for a flight to the Kyrgyz Republic; this permission number shall be inserted in item 18 of the FPL. Requests submitted in violation of items 3.–3.2.6 will not be considered. Requests may be submitted in English or Russian. Air traffic messages (ALR, RCF, FPL, DLA, CHG, CNL, DEP, ARR and others) must be sent to MC ATM of KR via AFS - UAFMZDZX and also to the ATS centres en-route and ARO of the landing aerodrome using the following addresses: Bishkek ACC UСFMZRZX; ARO Manas UСFMZTZX; Osh ACC UСFOZRZX; ARO Osh UСFOZTZX; ARO Issyk-Kul UCFLZTZX; ARO Karakol UCFKZTZX. Carriers without a Ground Handling Agreement, representatives or ground handling agents at JSC Manas International Airport when operating charter flights shall sign the Ground Handling Charge Note AHM-830 (sample GEN 1.2-12 to GEN 1.2-15), which reflects the terms of Article 8 of the Main Agreement AHM-810 on liability and indemnity at the airport of service.

GEN_1_2: Scheduled services into the State of Qatar require a bilateral or multilateral agreement with the Qatar Civil Aviation Authority (QCAA) and prior approval. Airlines intending to operate ILYUSHIN, TUPOLEV, ANTONOV or YAK types must provide QCAA with Certificates of Airworthiness, Insurance and Radio/Station License at least 72 hours before the intended operation. Designated airlines seeking to commence operations must submit for review and acceptance an official letter of intent, Air Operator's Certificate (AOC) and Operations Specification, Certificate of Airworthiness, Certificate of Registration, Insurance Certificate, Noise Certificate, Radio Certificate (Station License), a lease agreement where the aircraft is leased, GSA agreement and letter from the GSA, the proposed schedule and the Air Operator Security Programme (AOSP); upon acceptance QCAA will issue Advance Risk Assessment System (ARAS) APP/PNR documents to the airline. Applications must be submitted to Director of Air Transport, Civil Aviation Authority, P.O. BOX 3000, DOHA, STATE OF QATAR; Tel: (974) 4455 7333 Ext 320/316; Fax: (974) 4455 2233; AFS: OTBDYAYX, OTBDYFYX; SITA: DOHXYYF.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Operators intending non‑scheduled flights into, or transiting the State of Qatar must obtain prior landing/overflying permission from QCAA via https://caa.gov.qa/en/non-scheduled-flights-service; if the portal is unavailable contact Doha Communication on +974 4470 5081 or doha.comm@caa.gov.qa for manual processing. For non‑scheduled landings submit requests at least 72 hours before the intended flight including: name of operator, aircraft callsign, type of aircraft and MTOW, aircraft registration, date/time of arrival and departure at Doha International/Hamad International, places of embarkation/disembarkation abroad, purpose of flight and number of passengers and/or nature and amount of freight, billing email and address, name/address/business of charterer if any, and name and nationality of lead and all accompanying passengers; apply for schedule/slot approval in IATA Chapter 6 format to slots@qatarcoordination.com.qa with cc to airlines.affairs@caa.gov.qa and doha.comm@caa.gov.qa; ensure the following certificates are valid and carried on board at all times: certificate of registration, certificate of airworthiness, air operator’s certificate, certificate of insurance, radio station license and noise certificate; include the landing/overflying clearance number under RMK/ in Item 18 of the flight plan and ensure the operator name appears under OPR/ in Item 18; submit applications to the address specified under GEN 1.2 subsection 2.1.4 and comply with documentary and operational requirements specified under GEN 1.2 subsections 2.3 and 2.4. For overflying the territory of the State of Qatar obtain prior overflying permission at least 48 hours before the intended flight via the same web portal, providing name of operator, flight number/callsign, purpose of flight, nature of cargo, ATS route with entry/exit points of the Doha FIR, flight level and timings in UTC, and billing contact and email; for non‑scheduled transit (overflying the Doha FIR outside the territory of the State of Qatar) submit flight details via the portal using the “Flight Notification” service and QCAA will acknowledge receipt; comply with flight plan requirements in ENR 1.10 and ENR 1.11, ensure OPR/ is included in Item 18, and follow the flight plan requirement referenced at GEN 1.2 subsection 3.6.3; contact Qatar Aviation Services (QAS) for all ground handling services as per GEN 1.1 subsection 9.

GEN_1_2: All flights into, from, or over Oman, and all landings within Oman, must comply with Oman civil aviation regulations. Aircraft landing in or departing from Oman must first land at and finally depart from Muscat International Airport (see AD 2.OOMS) unless a special dispensation has been applied for and granted in advance by the CAA.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Permission is required for all non-scheduled flights into, from or over the territory of Oman, whether or not for the purpose of taking on or discharging passengers, cargo or mail; applications for non-scheduled flight permission must be submitted to the CAA at the address given in GEN 1.2-1 paragraph 2.2 a) and must arrive not less than seventy-two hours before the intended landing or overflight. Applications for non-scheduled flights must include the following information in the order shown: name of operator and full mailing address including telephone/fax/e-mail; type of aircraft, MTOW, registration marks and availability of ACAS-II and serviceable transponder; callsign/flight number (ICAO 3 letter code); operator’s IATA Code; dates and times of arrival at and departure from Muscat Intl Airport and Salalah Airport; entry/exit points within Muscat FIR and itinerary schedule (ETD/ETA); place or places of embarkation or disembarkation abroad of passenger and/or freight; purpose of flight, number of passengers and/or nature and amount of freight; name, address and business of charterer if any; names of crew members; valid and clear copies of the following documents (not required for private and overflying flights and/or technical stops): air operator certificate with the specifications, certificate of air worthiness, certificate of registration, certificate of insurance, aircraft noise certificate and radio licenses certificate; and responsible authority for air navigation, landing and parking charges including head of accounts dept. with full mailing address, account payable and telephone/fax and e-mail address. All non-scheduled aircraft overflying the Sultanate of Oman and operating into Muscat Intl and Salalah airports shall quote the permit number granted on each and every Flight Plan. Private flights require permission and applications must be submitted in accordance with paragraph 3.2 and addressed to the Directorate of Air Transport at the address given in sub-paragraph 2.2 (b); no copies of certificates are required for private flight applications.

GEN_1_2: International flights to, from, or over Singapore are subject to current Singapore civil aviation regulations, which correspond in all essentials to ICAO Annex 9; aircraft flying into or departing Singapore shall make their first landing at, or final departure from, an international aerodrome (see AIP Singapore AD 1.3-1 and AD 2). Operators must consult the AIPs of States along planned routes for any documentary or permit requirements for entry, transit or overflight, and for Indonesian sovereign airspace within portions where Singapore provides ATS (see ENR 2.1) operators must consult AIP Indonesia GEN 1.2 Entry, Transit and Departure of Aircraft at https://iwish.kemenhub.go.id/ for Indonesia's requirements.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: All non‑scheduled operations require prior permission: overflights by civil aircraft may be facilitated provided adequate advance notification is given; notification by flight plan to Singapore ATC Centre (WSJCZQZX) received at least 2 hours before arrival into the Singapore FIR will normally be accepted, otherwise diplomatic clearance must be obtained from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Non‑traffic or technical landings also require prior notification; a flight plan to WSJCZQZX received at least 2 hours before arrival at Singapore Changi or Seletar or 2 hours prior to entering the Singapore FIR (whichever is earlier) will normally be accepted, otherwise diplomatic permission via the Ministry of Foreign Affairs is required. All non‑scheduled traffic landings/uplifts require prior approval and only the operator or authorised agency may apply via https://flightsg.caas.gov.sg; normal permit applications and supporting documents must reach CAAS Air Transport Division at least 3 whole working days before the first flight (a working day is 08:30–18:00 Mon–Thu and 08:30–17:30 Fri; applications after close are deemed received next working day), express permit applications may be considered if submitted at least 24 hours before the flight and the Air Transport Duty Officer is to be alerted at +65 9833 1775; applications with insufficient notice will not be considered except for emergency flights (e.g. humanitarian). Application data must include operator name, address and nationality; charterer name, address and business; aircraft type, registration and carrying capacity; aircraft documents (see para 4.2.5); nature of flight and passenger or cargo details (for passenger flights: origin/destination points, purpose and passenger names; for cargo flights: origin/destination, description, quantities and dimensions, outbound/inbound/transhipment status and whether items are perishable or dangerous); route, points of landing and final destination; arrival/departure dates and times; operator’s local agent and ground handling agent contact details; consignees/consignors where applicable; and any other relevant information. Documentary requirements for clearance are the same as for scheduled flights and CAAS may attach conditions to any permit. Permit fees for normal permits (apply at least 3 whole working days before first flight) are S$84 for a single one‑way or return flight, S$162 for 2–5 one‑way/return flights, S$326 for 6–10 one‑way/return flights, and S$810 for more than 10; express permit fee (application at least 24 hours before flight) is S$252 for a single one‑way or return flight. Operators (except business aviation operators) should schedule Changi arrivals/departures outside 0001–0200 UTC (0801–1000 LT) and 0900–1559 UTC (1700–2359 LT); aircraft may remain on the ground during those times subject to approval and stand availability but must vacate the stand if required. Business aviation/executive jet charters may be permitted to remain or layover at Changi; all business aviation aircraft must park nose‑in, be pushed back using an aircraft tow‑bar and tow‑tractor (reverse thrust and variable‑pitch propellers must not be used), and the operator must provide or arrange an appropriate tow‑bar with the ground handling agent; all business aviation flights must engage a ground handling agent at Changi, all passengers must clear CIQ via the Commercially‑Important‑Persons (CIP) facility adjacent to Terminal 2, and requests to handle executive or charter flights via main terminals are to be emailed to csc@changiairport.com for exceptional consideration. All non‑traffic aircraft must submit a copy of the Certificate of Airworthiness to CAAS after each landing by fax to 6545 6519 or by email to CAAS_FS_FOS@caas.gov.sg. Foreign State aircraft must obtain diplomatic clearance from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, provided at least 14 days’ notice, and applications must include mission/organisation, liaison officer and telephone number, number and type of aircraft, callsign, registration, full flight itinerary and route in/out of Singapore FIR, dates and times of arrival/departure, arrival from/departing to, requested airfield, pilot name, number of crew and passengers, VIP names and officials if applicable, purpose, photographic/sensor equipment if any, nature of freight, dangerous cargoes if any, types of services required (fuel/APU/GPU/ground handling) and any special requests; aircraft used in military, customs or police services are deemed State aircraft. Operating contrary to the non‑scheduled flight permit requirements may result in fines and/or imprisonment under applicable law.

airports.AD2.2 Remarks: a. Not available to all non-scheduled civil aircraft types of 40-seater or below except in special circumstances. Aircraft larger than the above category shall not plan their arrival between 09:00-1559UTC. b. Aircraft shall leave nose-in position (90 degrees) with the aid of aircraft tow tractors. Reverse thrust or variable pitch propellers shall not be used. Aircraft operators shall make suitable arrangements. c. Prior permission required for aircraft not equipped with radiotelephony. d. A subsonic jet aircraft, unless otherwise exempted, is not permitted to operate in Singapore unless it possesses a noise certificate stating that it meets the noise standards of ICAO Annex 16, Volume 1, Chapter 3, or equivalent. The noise certificate may also take the form of a suitable statement contained in another document approved by the State of Registry of the aircraft. e. RVR minima for CATII ILS operations is limited to 350m due to runway and taxiway light spacing requirements on the airfield. f. Frangible poles are installed for the purpose of identifying 90m away from the centreline of RWY 02L/20R and RWY 02C/20C

GEN_1_2: An international flight is a flight performed in the airspaces of any two or more countries. Flights in Mongolian airspace are classified as scheduled and non‑scheduled. Scheduled and non‑scheduled flights shall operate only with a flight permit issued by the Civil Aviation Authority of Mongolia (CAA); the Air Traffic Flow Management Division (ATFMD) receives flight requests and related documents, implements air traffic flow management per procedures and issues permits on behalf of the CAA. Flight traffic, airspace and airport capacity shall be considered when planning and issuing permits, and prior to departure the flight applicant must submit the flight plan to ATFMD and other relevant departments in accordance with ENR 1.10 and ENR 1.11. Permissions are valid for 72 hours for international flights, 48 hours for domestic flights and 24 hours for special flights, and overflight permission remains valid during an emergency landing. The flight applicant is responsible for the accuracy, validity and timely updating of all flight plans, flight requests and related documents. Requests for foreign special‑duty and official flights shall be submitted via the state central administrative body responsible for foreign affairs, and requests for domestic special‑duty and official flights via the State Special Protection Agency; state aircraft operations are subject to the Law on the Use of Airspace in Flight, the Law on State Aviation and joint procedures for special‑duty/state aircraft traffic. Flights near or across borders must comply with the Law on Borders of Mongolia, the Law on the Use of Airspace in Flight and the Mongolian‑Chinese and Russian‑Mongolian border regime agreements and other applicable rules; flights in protected areas are subject to Article 12(8) of the Law on Special Protected Areas. Non‑scheduled local commercial flight requests from foreign operators are subject to the applicable intergovernmental Air Services Agreement, and non‑scheduled international take‑off and landing requests will be assessed considering airspace and airport capacity and ground handling; if approved, date and time may be adjusted to accommodate these factors. A flight permit may be refused for incomplete, inconsistent, expired or invalid documents, insufficient airspace or airport capacity, or other factors adversely affecting safety, and the CAA is not responsible for consequences to the applicant arising from such refusal due to non‑compliance. ATFMD top management contact: Tel: 976-71285086 (Director).
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Non-scheduled flights (any flights other than scheduled) require prior permission from the Air Traffic Flow Management Division; requests are processed by the Flight Slot and Permit Section (Tel: 976-71282029 and 976-71282213 for international, 976-71282235 for domestic; Fax: 976-70049981; E-mail: fpd@mcaa.gov.mn) and by the Tactical Planning Section (AFS: ZMUBZGZX, ZMUBYAYX; Tel: 976-71281602; Fax: 976-70049680; E-mail: tpu@mcaa.gov.mn). Non-scheduled international flight requests must be submitted on Form B. Special and VIP international flight requests on Form B must be received at least 2 calendar days before the start of operations. International overflight, take-off and landing requests on Form B or containing the information in Form B must be submitted at least 2 working days before the start of operations. If an international request is submitted 1 calendar day before operations the air navigation fee will be charged as an extra charge and the permit will be issued; if submitted at least 6 hours before operations the air navigation fee will be charged as a double extra charge and the permit will be issued. Requests for other international operations (other aircraft, transport of passengers, delivery of spare parts, navigation equipment, technical inspection for special duty, humanitarian, ambulance or in case of aircraft failure) on Form B must be submitted within 30 minutes prior to the start of overflight operations and within 1 hour prior to the start of take-off and landing operations. Non-scheduled domestic flight requests use Form D: special civil aircraft flights must be submitted at least 1 calendar day before operations; special state aircraft flights must be submitted at least 2 days before operations; requests for other domestic operations (other aircraft, transport of passengers, delivery of spare parts, navigation equipment, technical inspection for failure, special duty, humanitarian, ambulance) on Form D must be submitted within 30 minutes prior to the start of operations; non-scheduled domestic flight requests generally must be submitted at least 2 hours before the start of operations. Requests for five or more regular changes or additional flights to approved international and domestic scheduled take-off and landing schedules must be submitted to the Schedule Board. For overflight changes, adding a single flight to/from scheduled overflight planning requires Form A or the information included in it at least 2 working days before operations; one-time changes and cancellation notifications may be submitted as required; two or more regular changes and additional flight requests to scheduled overflight planning require Form A or the information included in it at least 5 working days before operations. For take-off and landing flight changes, a single change requires submission with relevant explanation at least 1 hour before operations, and 2–4 changes or requests for additional flights require Form A at least 5 working days before the start of operations.

GEN_1_2: All flights into, from, over or landing in Pakistan must comply with Pakistan’s National Regulations and any multilateral or bilateral agreements to which Pakistan is party; foreign civil aircraft registered in ICAO member states may operate in Pakistan subject to the rules, conditions and limitations in this document and the legislation referenced in paragraph 3, and aircraft of non‑ICAO states may operate only where reciprocal treatment to Pakistan aircraft and airmen exists and subject to the same rules. Israeli‑registered aircraft and flights of international airlines operating to or from Israel are prohibited from operating to or overflying Pakistan. Aircraft arriving in or departing from Pakistan must first land at or finally depart from an international airport, and after landing must not proceed until the pilot has completed customs and other formalities and obtained a written clearance from an officer authorized by the Federal Government; if compelled to land at a non‑international aerodrome the pilot‑in‑command or their representative must immediately contact the nearest customs official or police, and any charge for dispatching a customs officer to an emergency landing will be charged to the aircraft owner. Non‑scheduled aircraft flying over or across Pakistan may be required by the Federal Government to land at an international airport on both outward and inward journeys and may only proceed after issuance of a clearance certificate by an authorized officer; except as provided above, only aircraft engaged in scheduled air transport may operate into or transit across Pakistan. All civil aircraft overflying Pakistan are prohibited from carrying arms, ammunition of war or explosives except those authorized for navigational or signaling purposes, prohibited from operating in a manner likely to endanger or interfere with Pakistani forces, ships or aircraft, and must clearly display nationality and registration marks as prescribed.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: All international non‑scheduled flight permissions must be obtained through Authorized Flight Permission Agents (AFPAs) in accordance with ANO-003-ATNR; the ANO and the list/contacts of AFPAs are available at www.caapakistan.com.pk. The operator must designate a CAA‑approved local authorized agent to coordinate with the Operations Directorate and ATC operational units; permission for prolonged stay is issued for 90 days and must be reacquired after that period; prolonged parking is permitted only at Karachi/Jinnah International Airport, Lahore/Allama Iqbal International Airport and Islamabad/Islamabad International Airport. Applications must include certified true copies of: Aircraft Operating Certificate (AOC); Certificate of Registration; Certificate of Airworthiness; Insurance Policy; Radio License; Noise Certificate; Operations Specifications; Flight Crew License; Weight and Balance Approval; and, if available, the audit report of the Ground Handler/Air Operator. Authorized agents must ensure valid travel documents for all operating crew and be prepared to produce copies; crews on short‑term stay shall operate on a General Declaration (GD) and appropriate visas must be obtained for prolonged stays. Clearance is issued on a first‑come, first‑serve basis after acceptance of all documents. Director Airworthiness/Director Flight Standards may, via the Director Air Transport and Economic Regulations, hold operations following thorough inspection of the aircraft or documents. Allow 96 hours for initial document scrutiny.

GEN_1_2: All flights into, from, or over the territory of the Republic of Korea and landing in such territory must be conducted in accordance with the Republic of Korea civil aviation regulations. All aircraft landing in or departing from the Republic of Korea must first land at or finally depart from the designated international airport. All flights operating over the territory and adjacent waters must file a flight plan with the Air Traffic Management Office and the Incheon Air Traffic Control Regional Office prior to the intended flight.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Foreign operators planning non-scheduled commercial international flights with foreign‑registered aircraft must obtain Foreign Non‑scheduled International Air Transport Permission from the International Air Transport Division, Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport (Tel: +82-44-201-4210) at least 10 days before the intended operation (16 days for military airports); the application must include operator nationality and name, aircraft nationality and registration mark, aircraft type, flight identification/number, route(s) of flight, date and time of operation, segments of revenue flight, purpose of the transport, pilot‑in‑command name and number of flight crews, passenger names and nationalities or cargo description with number/weight, type and amount of fare, and any other items required by the Ministry. Foreign operators planning non‑commercial or private international flights with foreign‑registered aircraft must submit an application to the Administrator of the Regional Office of Aviation, Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport at least 2 days before the intended operation (16 days for military airports); the application must include operator name, address and nationality, aircraft nationality and registration mark, type and flight identification/number, route of flight with each airport of call clearly listed, date and time of flight, purpose of flight, pilot‑in‑command name and names and qualifications of crew members, passenger names and nationalities and purpose of travel, and a cargo manifest. Operations of foreign State Aircraft must be applied for through the diplomatic channel to the Administrator of the Regional Office of Aviation at least 2 days before the intended operation (16 days for military airports) and require the same documentation as for Scheduled flights. For quarantine and clearance, upon arrival the aircraft captain or crew chief must ensure the Flight Declaration of Health (Exit/Entry), Crew and Passengers List, health questionnaires and Declaration of Health are completed and available prior to release of passengers and must cooperate with health inspections of in‑flight facilities and meals; review of the need for disinfection prior to arrival is not required and disinfection after arrival does not prevent crew and passengers from disembarking.

GEN_1_2: International flights into, from, or over Myanmar are subject to current Myanmar civil aviation regulations, which correspond in all essential respects to the Standards and Recommended Practices of ICAO Annex 9; aircraft operating into or departing from Myanmar must make their first landing at, or final departure from, an International Aerodrome (see AIP Myanmar AD1.3, AD2).
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: For non‑scheduled overflights, operators must apply to the Director General, Department of Civil Aviation, Yangon International Airport, Yangon 11021, Myanmar (Fax: 951 533008 / 533016; AFTN: VYYYYAYX; E‑mail: ats@dca.gov.mm) at least 7 days before the effective date and provide operator name and mailing/billing address, aircraft type and registration marks, full itinerary including point of departure and destination with date/time of overflight, purpose of overflight (passenger or cargo) and declaration of cargo/freight if applicable, consignee/consignor or charterer, and fax number, SITA address, AFTN address and contact person; for non‑scheduled landing flights submit the same information plus local sponsor name and address and request landing permission at least 14 days in advance, providing passenger‑charter lists with names and nationalities when applicable or declaring type and nature of cargo together with consignee/consignor for cargo‑charter flights; documentary requirements are the same requirements as for SCHEDULED FLIGHTS and no public health measures such as disinfecting are required for aircraft entering Myanmar; private flights requesting overflight clearance must comply with the non‑scheduled overflight requirements, private flights requesting landing clearance must request permission to land at Yangon International Airport at least 7 days in advance and at other airports at least 14 days in advance, and private medical evacuation flights must include local sponsor name, address and phone/fax, patient information (name, disease, age, current hospital, attending doctor, organization), aircraft operator and mailing address, aircraft type and registration, flight schedule date/time, and will be given top priority.

airports.AD2.2 Remarks: 1.    Atterraggi e decolli su LIMB AD sono consentiti solo per voli da/ per stati membri UE e stati extra-UE aderenti all’area Schengen 2. Il modulo di notifica deve essere inoltrato: a.       almeno 12 ore prima dell’ EOBT/ETA per voli da/per stati membri UE e non aderenti all’aerea Shengen e da/per stati extra-UE aderenti all’area Schengen b.       almeno 3 ore prima dell’ EOBT/ETA per tutti gli altri voli c.       voli non interessati: voli di Stato, HEMS, voli di addestramento effettuati sia dai membri di associazioni di volo che da compagnie concessionarie, basati su LIMB, con aeromobili sia di proprietà che usualmente di base a LIMB 4.    ACB tel +39 02 6101625/66508616/66508612 fax +39 02 66505959 1.    Landing and take-off on LIMB AD allowed only for flights to/ from EU member States and non-EU States member of the Schengen area 2.    All flights to/from LIMB shall be notified to Aeroclub Milano by means of a specific form available at www.aeroclubmilano.it 3.    The notification form shall be forwarded:

GEN_1_2: All flights entering, overflying, or landing in the United States must comply with U.S. civil aviation regulations. Aircraft arriving in or departing from the United States are required to first land at, or finally depart from, an international airport as specified in AD 2, unless otherwise authorized. Aircraft entering the United States must land at a designated international airport of entry unless prior landing rights approval has been obtained from U.S. Customs for a landing rights or other authorized airport, and all persons entering the United States are subject to Customs, Immigration, and Public Health inspection. Foreign civil aircraft registered in ICAO member states may operate within the United States in accordance with applicable Federal Aviation Regulations and Department of Transportation requirements, while aircraft registered in non-ICAO member states require specific authorization from the Department of Transportation. All foreign civil aircraft must carry valid certificates of registration and airworthiness, and each flight crew member must hold an appropriate and valid license. Transportation of firearms and ammunition by passengers is subject to U.S. federal regulations requiring written notice to the carrier unless such items are placed in the custody of the aircraft operator for the duration of the flight. Commercial air transport operators must comply with ICAO Annex 6 fuel reserve requirements, including a minimum fixed reserve of 45 minutes plus a 15 percent variable reserve to destination and alternate when required, or a maximum reserve of two hours fuel where applicable.
GEN_1_2_POINT_4: Private aircraft operating to, from, within, or transiting U.S. territorial airspace must comply with applicable security requirements issued under 14 CFR 99.7 and related FAA Special Security Instructions. Operators conducting private flights with intermediate landings in the United States must provide advance notice of arrival to U.S. Customs at or nearest the first intended airport of landing, with sufficient time to allow inspection officials to be present; at least one hour notice is required during normal business hours, and additional time may be required outside those hours. The notification must include aircraft type and registration, name of the aircraft commander, number of U.S. citizen and non-U.S. citizen passengers, last foreign departure point, estimated time and location of U.S. border or coastline crossing, intended first landing airport, and estimated time of arrival. Advance notice may be transmitted via an ADCUS message in the flight plan where available; however, the pilot remains fully responsible for ensuring Customs is properly notified and may be subject to penalties for noncompliance. Additional one-hour advance notification requirements apply for certain arrivals from Mexico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Puerto Rico, and specified southern border, Gulf, Atlantic, and Pacific ADIZ areas, and aircraft subject to these provisions must land for inspection at the nearest designated airport unless an approved overflight exemption has been granted. Private aircraft are defined as civil aircraft not engaged in the transportation of persons or property for compensation or hire. Operators may apply to the appropriate U.S. Customs authority for single or term overflight exemptions from the designated airport landing requirement, subject to advance application timelines and compliance with operating conditions including minimum altitude requirements, approved crew and passenger identification, aircraft capability and equipment specifications, approved first landing airports, and full disclosure of ownership, operational, and routing details; conditional approvals may apply to charter or air taxi operators, and incomplete applications will not be processed.

airports.AD2.2 Remarks:
OMDB operates as an IATA level 3 slot coordinated airport. No operator shall operate to or from OMDB without first obtaining an allocated slot from Airport Coordination Limited (ACL) and subject to landing permission from the DCAA. Schedules should be sent in IATA SSIM format to ACL in the time scales specified by the IATA schedules calendar to the address below: Email: slots@acl-international.com Fax: +44 (0) 208 564 0691
OMDB AD 2.3 OPERATIONAL HOURS

Daylight/VMC operations only.
Prior Permission Required. Contact Munawala Ground Handling
Aerodrome Reference Code 4C

OMDW operates as a IATA level 2 slot coordinated airport. No operator shall operate to or from OMDW without first obtaining clearance from Airport Coordination Limited (ACL) and subject to landing permission from the DCAA. Schedules should be sent in IATA SSIM format to ACL in the time scales specified by the IATA schedules calendar to the address below.
Email: slots@acl-international.com
FAX: +44 (0) 208 564 0691
Aircraft greater in size than ICAO Code F (Wingspan Greater than 80 M) must provide 72 hour advance notice to the aerodrome in addition to a slot request to ACL.
Email: Safeguarding-aim@dubaiairports.ae
EFTA operates to the South of OMDW RWY 12/30. EFTA RWY 13/31 is not available for commercial aircraft. Operators are to be aware of high intensity training activities in this area
airports.AD2.3 Remarks:
Prior Permission required from Dubai Airports and EFTA Operations for any Non-EFTA flights which are operating into EFTA (RWY 13/31).
EFTA operates as per the below timings:
01 APR – 30 SEP 0200 – 1800 UTC
01 OCT – 31 MAR 0300 – 1900 UTC
Note: There will be no flying outside the above timings.
